title: Reading and Use of English - Part 7
description: You are going to read an article about four people who discuss how and why their shopping or consumer habits have changed. For questions 43–52, choose from the people (A–D). The people may be chosen more than once.

content.title: Think Before You Buy: Four People on Changing Consumer Habits

texts.A:
### Simone, 29, PR Consultant
Working in PR means being surrounded by brands and products constantly, which made me a fairly unreflective consumer for most of my twenties. [43]I decided to stop buying new clothing for an entire year after reading a detailed report on the textile industry's environmental and human rights record – the figures were striking enough to change my behaviour immediately.[/43] I was genuinely surprised by how little I missed it in the end. [47]The hardest moments were social ones – weddings, events, situations where there was an unspoken expectation to present something new.[/47] I rented outfits for those occasions, which was more enjoyable than I expected. [51]The financial benefit was also unexpected – I saved more in that single year than in the previous three combined, and I've since invested those savings in genuinely high-quality, long-lasting pieces.[/51]
texts.B:
### Howard, 63, Professor of Economics
[44]I've studied consumer behaviour for four decades, and what strikes me about the current ethical consumerism movement is how much of it is driven by guilt rather than by a clear-eyed understanding of actual impact.[/44] I don't say that to dismiss the impulse – it is fundamentally positive – but the evidence for individual purchasing decisions driving systemic change is weaker than the marketing around it suggests. [48]I personally try to buy locally, seasonally and with as little packaging as possible, but I hold no illusion that my personal choices alone are solving a structural problem.[/48] [52]What I find most encouraging is the growing pressure on large corporations and governments from well-informed young consumers – that is where real leverage for change ultimately lies.[/52]
texts.C:
### Layla, 43, Nursery Manager
[45]Three years ago I did a full audit of my home and found I owned a large number of things I didn't use, couldn't remember buying and simply didn't need.[/45] It prompted a wholesale change in how I approach all purchases – I now apply a strict two-week waiting period before buying anything non-essential, which eliminates most impulse purchases immediately. [49]Running a nursery, I've also brought this thinking into my workplace, auditing equipment and resources regularly and resisting the urge to buy new when what we have still functions well.[/49] My colleagues were sceptical initially, but the cost savings have won them over completely.
texts.D:
### Stefan, 34, Fitness Instructor
[46]I used to spend an embarrassing amount of money on supplements, equipment and branded activewear, largely because the fitness industry is exceptionally good at making you feel that the right product will get you the results you want.[/46] It took years of professional experience to understand that fundamentals – adequate sleep, good nutrition and consistent training – matter infinitely more than any product. [50]I now spend almost nothing on fitness products beyond the absolute basics and instead direct that money towards continuing professional development and further qualifications.[/50] I find this makes me a more credible trainer – I tell clients honestly when something isn't necessary, and they appreciate it.

questions.question: changed their consumer behaviour as a direct response to information they read about an industry's practices?
questions.explanation: Simone stopped buying new clothes for a year after reading a report on the textile industry's environmental and human rights record.

questions.question: approaches ethical consumerism from a professionally sceptical, evidence-based standpoint?
questions.explanation: Howard, a professor of consumer behaviour, says the evidence for individual purchasing driving systemic change is weaker than the marketing suggests.

questions.question: discovered through a deliberate review of their possessions that they had accumulated many unnecessary items?
questions.explanation: Layla conducted a full home audit and found she owned things she didn't use, couldn't remember buying and simply didn't need.

questions.question: acknowledges having spent money on products that their professional knowledge later revealed were unnecessary?
questions.explanation: Stefan, a fitness instructor, admits spending embarrassing amounts on supplements and branded gear before his professional experience taught him fundamentals matter far more.

questions.question: found that social situations presented the most difficult obstacle to maintaining their new habits?
questions.explanation: Simone says the hardest moments were social ones, such as weddings and events, where there was an expectation to wear something new.

questions.question: is honest about the limited real-world impact of their own individual purchasing decisions?
questions.explanation: Howard says he buys locally and with minimal packaging but holds no illusion that his personal choices alone are solving a structural problem.

questions.question: has applied their changed approach to consumption in their workplace as well as in their private life?
questions.explanation: Layla has brought her approach of auditing resources and waiting before buying into the nursery she manages.

questions.question: has redirected money that was previously spent on products towards improving their own professional skills?
questions.explanation: Stefan now directs money formerly spent on fitness products towards continuing professional development and further qualifications.

questions.question: mentions a significant financial gain they had not anticipated as a result of changing their consumer habits?
questions.explanation: Simone says she saved more in the year she stopped buying new clothes than in the three years before combined.

questions.question: identifies a broader social or political force that they consider more capable of producing real change than individual choices?
questions.explanation: Howard says the most encouraging thing is the growing pressure on corporations and governments from well-informed young consumers, which is where real leverage lies.
